request.question: Which film director, Frank Tashlin or Craig Serling, was also an animator?

title: The Girl Can't Help It
Son of Paleface
Izzy Ellis
Craig Serling
Frank Tashlin
The Fuller Brush Girl
Susan Slept Here
The Bear That Wasn't
The Private Navy of Sgt. O'Farrell
The Good Humor Man (1950 film)
sentences: The Girl Can't Help It is a 1956 musical comedy starring Jayne Mansfield in the titular role, Tom Ewell, Edmond O'Brien, Henry Jones, and Julie London.
The picture was produced and directed by Frank Tashlin, with a screenplay adapted by Tashlin and Herbert Baker from an uncredited 1955 novel "Do Re Mi" by Garson Kanin.
The movie was originally intended as a vehicle for the American sex symbol Jayne Mansfield, with a satirical subplot involving teenagers and rock 'n' roll music.
The unintended result has been called the "most potent" celebration of rock music ever captured on film.
Son of Paleface is a 1952 Western comedy film directed by Frank Tashlin and starring Bob Hope, Jane Russell, and Roy Rogers.
The film is a sequel to "The Paleface" (1948).
Written by Tashlin, Joseph Quillan, and Robert L. Welch, the film is about a man who returns home to claim his father's gold, which is nowhere to be found.
"Son of Paleface" was released in the United States by Paramount Pictures on July 14, 1952.
Isadore Ellis (1910–1994), credited as I. Ellis and known throughout the animation industry as Izzy Ellis, was an American animator best known for working for Warner Bros.
Cartoons under the Bob Clampett & Frank Tashlin units.
Ellis had a long career in the animation industry, which started at Ub Iwerks Studio in 1935.
After three years, Ellis moved to Leon Schlesinger Productions, where he worked under the Clampett B/W unit, under the Ray Katz management.
Ellis continued to animate in the B/W unit under the supervision of Norman McCabe, and eventually Frank Tashlin.
Ellis reunited with Clampett after Robert McKimson took over Tashlin's unit.
However, Ellis relocated to McKimson's unit after Art Davis took over the Clampett unit.
In 1948, Ellis left to do commercial work.

Craig Serling is an American film director, film producer, screenwriter, and television editor known for multiple Primetime Emmy nominations for 'Outstanding Picture Editing for Non-Fiction Programming' and for his 2006 directorial debut feature film "Jam".
Francis Fredrick von Taschlein (February 19, 1913 – May 5, 1972), better known by his stage name Frank Tashlin, was an American animator, screenwriter, and film director.
He was also known as Tish Tash and Frank Tash.
The Fuller Brush Girl is a 1950 slapstick comedy starring Lucille Ball and directed by Lloyd Bacon.
Animator Frank Tashlin wrote the script.
Ball plays a quirky door-to-door cosmetics saleswoman for the Fuller Brush Company.
The film also stars Eddie Albert and has an uncredited cameo by Red Skelton (who had starred in the Tashlin-scripted "The Fuller Brush Man" two years earlier).
Susan Slept Here is a 1954 American romantic comedy film starring Dick Powell (in his last film role) and Debbie Reynolds.
Shot in Technicolor, the film was based on the play of the same name by Steve Fisher and Alex Gottlieb.
The film's plotline was later used again by director Frank Tashlin for 1962's "Bachelor Flat".
The Bear That Wasn't is a 1946 children's book by film director and "Looney Tunes" alumnus Frank Tashlin.
The Private Navy of Sgt. O'Farrell is a 1968 film directed by Frank Tashlin and starring Bob Hope, Phyllis Diller, and Jeffrey Hunter.
It was the final film for Tashlin, who died in 1972.
The Good Humor Man is a 1950 comedy crime film directed by Lloyd Bacon and written by Frank Tashlin.
The film revolves around a Good Humor ice cream salesman who becomes involved in a murder.
The film stars Jack Carson, Lola Albright, Jean Wallace, George Reeves, Peter Miles and Frank Ferguson.
The film was released on June 1, 1950, by Columbia Pictures.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Which film director, Frank Tashlin or Craig Serling, was also an animator?
hop1_docs: Frank Tashlin | Francis Fredrick von Taschlein (February 19, 1913 – May 5, 1972), better known by his stage name Frank Tashlin, was an American animator, screenwriter, and film director.  He was also known as Tish Tash and Frank Tash.

Craig Serling | Craig Serling is an American film director, film producer, screenwriter, and television editor known for multiple Primetime Emmy nominations for 'Outstanding Picture Editing for Non-Fiction Programming' and for his 2006 directorial debut feature film "Jam".

Izzy Ellis | Isadore Ellis (1910–1994), credited as I. Ellis and known throughout the animation industry as Izzy Ellis, was an American animator best known for working for Warner Bros.  Cartoons under the Bob Clampett & Frank Tashlin units.  Ellis had a long career in the animation industry, which started at Ub Iwerks Studio in 1935.  After three years, Ellis moved to Leon Schlesinger Productions, where he worked under the Clampett B/W unit, under the Ray Katz management.  Ellis continued to animate in the B/W unit under the supervision of Norman McCabe, and eventually Frank Tashlin.  Ellis reunited with Clampett after Robert McKimson took over Tashlin's unit.  However, Ellis relocated to McKimson's unit after Art Davis took over the Clampett unit.  In 1948, Ellis left to do commercial work.

The Fuller Brush Girl | The Fuller Brush Girl is a 1950 slapstick comedy starring Lucille Ball and directed by Lloyd Bacon.  Animator Frank Tashlin wrote the script.  Ball plays a quirky door-to-door cosmetics saleswoman for the Fuller Brush Company.  The film also stars Eddie Albert and has an uncredited cameo by Red Skelton (who had starred in the Tashlin-scripted "The Fuller Brush Man" two years earlier).

Porky in the North Woods | Porky in the North Woods (released December 19, 1936) is a Warner Brothers Looney Tunes short animated film.  The cartoon was supervised (directed) by Frank Tashlin (credited at "Frank Tash"), with animation by Volney White and Norman McCabe.  The musical score is by Carl Stalling.
Censored (film) | Censored is an animated short, directed by Frank Tashlin and first released in July 1944.  It is part of the Private Snafu series.
The Bear That Wasn't | The Bear That Wasn't is a 1946 children's book by film director and "Looney Tunes" alumnus Frank Tashlin.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Frank Tashlin: passage [1] explicitly calls him "American animator, screenwriter, and film director" and gives "(February 19, 1913 – May 5, 1972)". Passage [4] also refers to "Animator Frank Tashlin" (both quoted fragments from [1] and [4]).
  - Craig Serling: passage [2] lists him as "American film director, film producer, screenwriter, and television editor" (no "animator" token appears in [2]).
- Ambiguities:
  - Frank Tashlin appears with variants in the passages: "Frank Tashlin", "Francis Fredrick von Taschlein", and nicknames "Tish Tash" / "Frank Tash" (see [1], [5]). 
  - Craig Serling appears only as "Craig Serling" in the passages; no alternate pages/variants are shown.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entities: "Frank Tashlin" and "Craig Serling" (exact tokens as in passages).
  - Missing fact needed: an explicit statement (or reliable credits) on Craig Serling's page or filmography that he is credited as an "animator" (or definitive absence thereof). Frank Tashlin's animator role is already asserted in the passages.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Craig Serling" page — check the lede/infobox and the "Filmography" / "Career" / credits sections for the token "animator" or animator credits (search the page for "animator"). Example query: "Craig Serling animator" / "Craig Serling credits Jam animator".
  2) Open the "Frank Tashlin" page lede/infobox and the "Career"/"Animation" sections to confirm the animator attribution and corroborating credits (already present in provided passages; check for primary sources or film credits).
  3) If Craig Serling's page lacks detail, fetch external credits pages (e.g., "Craig Serling filmography", IMDb or specific film pages like "Jam (2006) – credits") to scan for any animator roles.
Final answer deferred until those target pages/sections are retrieved and checked for explicit animator credits.
hop2_docs: Craig Serling | Craig Serling is an American film director, film producer, screenwriter, and television editor known for multiple Primetime Emmy nominations for 'Outstanding Picture Editing for Non-Fiction Programming' and for his 2006 directorial debut feature film "Jam".

Jam (film) | Jam (or Jam: When Lives Collide) is a 2006 drama film directed by Craig E. Serling.  Written by Serling and Nicole Lonner, the film was executive produced by Dianne Burnett for Burnett Entertainment in association with Thanksgiving Films.  As Serling's first feature length project, "Jam" is based upon a short film by the same name that Serling shot in 2004.  Starring Elizabeth Bogush, Dan Byrd, Julie Claire, and David DeLuise, "Jam" premiered at the Vail Film Festival on April 1, 2006, aired on television on the Starz!  TV channel, and was released on DVD on July 3, 2007, by the Starz!  distribution branch of Anchor Bay Entertainment.

Twilight Zone: Rod Serling's Lost Classics | Twilight Zone: Rod Serling's Lost Classics was a 1994 telefilm consisting of two Rod Serling stories.  The film was co-produced by Serling's widow Carol Serling.  Reportedly, she found the two pieces in a trunk in the family's garage.

Craig Elliott | Craig Elliott (born July 13, 1971) is an illustrator, visual development artist and layout artist who works in the animation industry.  After graduating from the Art Center College of Design in Pasadena, California in 1996, he went on to work on numerous films for Disney Feature Animation, DreamWorks, Nickelodeon and Fox Animation Studios.  Craig also exhibits his fine art illustrations and paintings at both Wondercon and Comic-Con International as well as occasionally teaching at the Art Center College of Design and Gnomon School of Visual Effects.
Richard Serle | Richard Serle (fl.  1304–07), was an English politician.
Walter Serle | Walter Serle (fl.  1420) of Totnes, Devon, was an English politician.
Rod Serling's Triple W: Witches, Warlocks and Werewolves | Rod Serling's Triple W: Witches, Warlocks and Werewolves
hop2_titles: Twilight Zone: Rod Serling's Lost Classics
Rod Serling's Triple W: Witches, Warlocks and Werewolves
